\providecommand{\starversion}{unknown}
\InputIfFileExists{version.tex}{}{}

\title{\texttt{star\_reacher}: A Small, Deterministic Six-Degree-of-Freedom
  Space-Mission Simulator with Derived, Cited, and Validated Physical Models}
\author{Melvin Hoyer III}
% Version string instead of a calendar date keeps rebuilds of one revision
% textually identical (SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH reproducibility goal).
\date{Version \texttt{\starversion}}

\begin{document}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
\texttt{star\_reacher} is a deterministic six-degree-of-freedom space-mission
simulator for launch vehicles, satellites, and lunar and Mars missions, built
as a small C++17/Eigen compute core behind a Python analysis frontend. Its
design principle is scientific fidelity at minimal size: every physical model
carries a first-principles derivation, explicit domain-of-validity bounds,
and validation evidence in a published mathematical model library, and the
same inputs on the same binary always produce bit-identical outputs, gated in
continuous integration by cryptographic hash with no tolerance. The simulator
targets hardware from a Raspberry Pi 5 to a desktop workstation and carries
AI/ML navigation hooks --- a stepping API, seeded sensor emulation, and a
guidance-navigation-control plugin interface --- designed in from the first
commit. This report describes the architecture, the verification and
validation approach, and the intended applications. At the time of this
revision the project has completed its Phase~1 skeleton; results sections
state their status honestly and will accrete as the phased roadmap executes.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction and Related Tools}

High-fidelity astrodynamics and flight-simulation tools broadly divide into
heavyweight mission-analysis suites, whose models are difficult to audit end
to end, and approachable simulators that trade physical rigor for
accessibility. \texttt{star\_reacher} targets the gap between them: a small,
fully auditable, bit-reproducible truth-model simulator in which every logged
quantity traces to a derived and cited model with recorded validation
evidence. The project validates against established tools rather than
competing with them: NASA's GMAT is the primary cross-tool baseline, with
Orekit as the tie-breaker and as the primary baseline for models GMAT does
not implement. The classical astrodynamics on which the physical models rest
follows standard references, principally Vallado~\cite{vallado2013}.

\section{Architecture}

The system is split by a single boundary rule: everything inside the
deterministic time loop is C++, and everything before the initial epoch or
after the final epoch is Python. The C++17 core --- whose only mandatory
dependency is the Eigen linear-algebra library --- owns the equations of
motion, force and torque models, integrators, event detection, sensor models,
the pseudorandom-number streams, and the binary log writer; it never parses
text, touches the network, or reads the clock. The Python package owns
configuration validation and canonicalization, Monte Carlo orchestration, the
log loader and exporters, plotting, playback, and the documentation build.
The two halves meet at a pybind11 binding that passes a typed, hashed
configuration in and a self-describing versioned binary log (SRLOG) out; the
log header embeds the configuration hash, the binary's git hash, and the
master random seed, binding every output to its exact inputs. Determinism is
a contract, not an aspiration: the core loop is single-threaded with a fixed
evaluation order, floating-point contraction and fast-math optimizations are
disabled, and same-platform bit-identity is enforced in continuous
integration by comparing SHA-256 hashes of repeated runs.

\section{Physical Models}

Every physical model is documented in the companion mathematical model
library, where each chapter follows a fixed template: purpose, assumptions
and domain of validity, complete derivation with citations, discretization
and implementation notes with equation-label-to-code traceability, a
validation-evidence table of machine-checked test identifiers, and
references. A manifest lint enforces accretion: a model module without its
chapter fails the build. As of this revision the implemented model set
comprises the Phase~1 two-body point-mass placeholder, integrated with the
classical fourth-order Runge--Kutta method; its gravitational parameter is
taken from the IERS Conventions (2010)~\cite{iers2010}. The production model
set --- time systems, reference frames, ephemerides, harmonic gravity,
third-body perturbations, solar radiation pressure, atmospheres and drag,
vehicle mass properties and propulsion, and rigid-body attitude dynamics ---
lands in the later phases of the roadmap and will be summarized here as each
phase completes.

\section{Verification and Validation Approach}

Verification and validation proceed in seven machine-checkable layers:
golden-vector unit tests per module, committed before implementation and
carrying provenance manifests; property-invariant tests such as energy and
angular-momentum conservation; analytic benchmarks against closed-form
solutions; frozen cross-tool comparison cases; determinism gates, both
per-commit bit-identity and a bounded cross-platform tolerance; seeded Monte
Carlo regression with distributional bounds; and performance regression on
pinned runners. A single acceptance command, \texttt{star verify}, runs the
acceptance subset and ends in an unambiguous pass or fail verdict with
failing test identifiers. Estimation algorithms introduced in later phases
are additionally gated on ensemble normalized-estimation-error-squared and
normalized-innovation-squared consistency statistics. Cross-tool validation
results will be reported in this section as the corresponding phases
complete; none have been executed at this revision.

\section{Case Studies}

The planned case studies --- a translunar transfer and an Earth--Mars
transfer, each run from committed mission definitions with script-regenerated
figures --- require the vehicle, environment, and data-output capabilities of
Phases~3 through~5 and have not yet been executed; this section will present
them when those phases complete.

\section{Applications and Future Work}

The simulator is built as a research instrument for three uses: conventional
mission analysis, guidance-navigation-control algorithm development against a
scrutinized truth model, and world-model and machine-learning spacecraft
navigation research. The AI/ML surface is present in the architecture from
the outset: a major-cycle stepping API suitable for reinforcement-learning
loops, seeded sensor emulation, a uniform plugin interface that accepts
Python and C++ components interchangeably, consistency tooling for learned
and classical estimators alike, and manifest-driven batch generation whose
NumPy export is the training-data interchange. Future work follows the
phased roadmap through sensors, plugins, and the Monte Carlo and
machine-learning layers, culminating in a validation campaign and a completed
version of this report; experimental results from the machine-learning hooks
will be reported once those layers exist.

\section{Conclusion}

\texttt{star\_reacher} pursues a deliberately narrow thesis: that a
six-degree-of-freedom space-mission simulator can be simultaneously small,
fully auditable, and reproducible to the bit, without sacrificing scientific
fidelity, and that such an instrument is the right substrate for
machine-learning navigation research because every training signal it emits
traces to a derived, cited, and tested model. The Phase~1 skeleton
establishes the contracts --- the deterministic core, the versioned log, the
documentation accretion gates, and the verification harness --- on which the
remaining phases build.

\printbibliography

\end{document}
